assumptions: The question is assessed for consolidated financial statements, not separate or individual financial statements.
The intragroup dividend has already been recognised as a receivable/payable, so the fact pattern is a recognised item rather than a forecast transaction.
The potential foreign exchange effect arises because the relevant group entities have different functional currencies.
No facts indicate that the receivable is being analysed as part of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: General intragroup prohibition in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must involve an external party. Intragroup items are normally not eligible there, except where IFRS 9 provides a specific exception.
approaches.practical_implication: An intercompany dividend receivable is not automatically hedgeable in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to external-party exposures, subject to stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Fair value hedge of FX risk on a recognized intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify as a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if the resulting FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The dividend receivable/payable is a monetary intragroup item
The counterparties have different functional currencies
The FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation under IAS 21
The hedge is formally designated and meets IFRS 9 hedge effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: A hedge may be possible in consolidation for the residual FX exposure on the booked intercompany dividend balance.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits FX risk on an intragroup monetary item in consolidation when the FX exposure is not fully eliminated.
Paragraph 6.5.2(a) places a hedge of a recognised asset or liability for a particular risk within the fair value hedge model.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness criteria.

approaches.label: Designation of only the foreign currency risk component
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits designation of a component of an item, including a specific risk, if that component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable. This supports hedging only the FX component once the item itself is eligible.
conditions: The underlying intercompany item is eligible as a hedged item
The foreign currency risk component is separately identifiable
The foreign currency risk component is reliably measurable
approaches.practical_implication: The designation can be limited to exchange risk rather than the entire dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.7 allows a component of an item, including a risk component, to be designated as the hedged item.
Paragraph B6.3.8 requires the risk component to be separately identifiable and reliably measurable.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: The forecast intragroup exception applies to highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. That does not match a dividend already recognised as receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: This route does not justify hedge accounting for the current booked dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows certain forecast intragroup transactions to qualify only when foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraph 6.3.3 requires a forecast transaction to be highly probable, and paragraph 6.5.2(b) frames it within the cash flow hedge model.
Paragraph B6.3.5 illustrates that only forecast intragroup transactions affecting consolidated profit or loss can qualify.

approaches.label: Hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: A net investment hedge is a separate hedge accounting model for foreign operations and cannot be applied by analogy to other exposures. A dividend receivable is not, on the stated facts, the net investment itself.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not analyse the booked intercompany dividend receivable under the net investment hedge model unless the exposure is genuinely the net investment.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7-8 limit the Interpretation to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and prohibit analogy to other hedge types.
Paragraphs 10-14 describe the specific hedged risk, hedged item and instrument location rules for net investment hedges.
Paragraph 6.5.2(c) identifies hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a distinct hedge type.
